name: Orion
myth: The Hunter, Greek mythology's giant huntsman placed among the stars by Zeus. The three belt stars are one of the most recognizable patterns.
keyStars: Orion Nebula (M42) visible below the belt
funFact: The Orion Nebula is ~1,344 ly away and contains enough gas to make ~10,000 stars.

name: Ursa Major
myth: The Great Bear, Contains the Big Dipper asterism. Dubhe and Merak point to Polaris.
funFact: Five of the Big Dipper stars actually move together through space, a "moving group."

name: Ursa Minor
myth: The Little Bear, Contains the North Star (Polaris), within 1° of the celestial north pole.

name: Cassiopeia
myth: The Queen, Ethiopian queen Cassiopeia, chained to her throne in the sky as punishment for her vanity.

name: Cygnus
myth: The Swan, Zeus disguised as a swan. Contains the Northern Cross asterism and lies in the Milky Way.
funFact: Deneb is so luminous that if placed where Sirius is, it would cast shadows.

name: Scorpius
myth: The Scorpion, Sent by Artemis to kill Orion; they are placed on opposite sides of the sky, never visible together.
funFact: Antares is so large that if placed at the Sun's position, it would extend past Mars' orbit.

name: Leo
myth: The Lion, The Nemean Lion slain by Hercules. Regulus marks its heart.

name: Gemini
myth: The Twins, Castor and Pollux, the Dioscuri of Greek mythology. Pollux is actually brighter despite being "beta."

name: Taurus
myth: The Bull, Zeus disguised as a white bull to abduct Europa. Contains the Pleiades and Hyades star clusters.
objects: Crab Nebula (M1), supernova remnant from 1054 AD
funFact: The Pleiades have been recognized by virtually every culture on Earth throughout history.

name: Canis Major
myth: The Great Dog, Orion's faithful hunting dog. Contains Sirius, the brightest star in the night sky.
keyStars: Sirius (-1.46 mag, 8.6 ly away, brightest star)
funFact: Ancient Egyptians based their calendar on Sirius' heliacal rising, which predicted the Nile floods.

name: Canis Minor
myth: The Lesser Dog, Orion's smaller hunting companion. Only two main stars.

name: Aquila
myth: The Eagle, Carried Zeus' thunderbolts. Altair forms the Summer Triangle with Vega and Deneb.
keyStars: Altair (one of closest bright stars, 16.7 ly, rapid rotator)

name: Lyra
myth: The Lyre, Orpheus' instrument. Vega was the North Star ~12,000 years ago and will be again in ~12,000 years.
funFact: Vega was the first star (after the Sun) to be photographed, in 1850.

name: Pegasus
myth: The Winged Horse, Born from Medusa's blood. The Great Square is easy to spot.

name: Andromeda
myth: The Chained Princess, Daughter of Cassiopeia, rescued by Perseus from the sea monster Cetus.
objects: Andromeda Galaxy (M31), nearest major galaxy, visible to naked eye, 2.5M ly away
funFact: The Andromeda Galaxy is approaching us at 110 km/s and will merge with the Milky Way in ~4.5 billion years.

name: Perseus
myth: The Hero, Slayer of Medusa. Contains the "Demon Star" Algol, an eclipsing binary that dims every 2.87 days.

name: Sagittarius
myth: The Archer, A centaur aiming at Scorpius. The Milky Way's center lies in this direction.
funFact: Looking toward Sagittarius, you're looking at the center of our galaxy, ~26,000 ly away.

name: Capricornus
myth: The Sea-Goat, A goat-fish hybrid, associated with Pan in Greek mythology.

name: Aquarius
myth: The Water Bearer, Ganymede, cupbearer to the gods. An ancient constellation recognized across cultures.

name: Pisces
myth: The Fishes, Aphrodite and Eros transformed into fish, tied together by a cord.

name: Aries
myth: The Ram, The golden-fleeced ram of Greek mythology. Once contained the vernal equinox.

name: Cancer
myth: The Crab, Sent by Hera to distract Hercules. Faint but contains the Beehive Cluster.

name: Virgo
myth: The Maiden, Goddess of harvest. Contains the Virgo Cluster with ~2,000 galaxies.
funFact: The Virgo Cluster is the heart of the Virgo Supercluster, our cosmic address.

name: Libra
myth: The Scales, Originally the claws of Scorpius. The only zodiac constellation representing an object, not a creature.

name: Crux
myth: The Southern Cross, Smallest constellation but iconic. Used for navigation in the Southern Hemisphere.
funFact: Crux was visible from Mediterranean latitudes 2,000 years ago due to precession.

name: Centaurus
myth: The Centaur, Chiron, the wise centaur teacher of Greek heroes. Contains the closest star system to our Sun.
keyStars: Alpha Centauri (4.37 ly, closest star system)

name: Draco
myth: The Dragon, Ladon, guardian of the golden apples. Thuban was the North Star when the pyramids were built.

name: Hercules
myth: The Hero, Greatest of Greek heroes. Contains the famous Keystone asterism and a great globular cluster.

name: Boötes
myth: The Herdsman, Follows Ursa Major around the pole. Arcturus guided Polynesian navigators.
keyStars: Arcturus (brightest star in N hemisphere, orange giant)
funFact: Arcturus was the first star whose motion across the sky was detected, by Edmond Halley in 1718.

name: Corona Borealis
myth: The Northern Crown, Ariadne's crown, given by Dionysus and placed among the stars.

name: Corvus
myth: The Crow, Apollo's sacred bird. A compact trapezoid easily spotted south of Virgo.

name: Crater
myth: The Cup, Apollo's goblet, placed in the sky alongside the Crow.

name: Hydra
myth: The Water Snake, The longest constellation, spanning over 100°. Alphard means "the solitary one."
keyStars: Alphard (orange giant, brightest in a barren region)

name: Lupus
myth: The Wolf, An animal impaled on a spear by Centaurus. Rich Milky Way star fields.

name: Ara
myth: The Altar, The altar of the gods upon which oaths were sworn before the war against the Titans.

name: Pavo
myth: The Peacock, Hera's sacred bird with Argus' hundred eyes on its tail.

name: Tucana
myth: The Toucan, A South American bird. Contains the Small Magellanic Cloud.

name: Phoenix
myth: The Phoenix, The mythical bird reborn from its ashes.

name: Grus
myth: The Crane, A wading bird of the southern sky.

name: Carina
myth: The Keel, Part of the ancient ship Argo Navis. Contains Canopus, the 2nd brightest star.
funFact: The Carina Nebula contains Eta Carinae, one of the most massive and luminous stars known.

name: Vela
myth: The Sails, Sails of the ship Argo Navis.

name: Puppis
myth: The Stern, The poop deck of Argo Navis.

name: Columba
myth: The Dove, Noah's dove, or the dove sent by the Argonauts.

name: Lepus
myth: The Hare, A hare at Orion's feet, hunted by his dogs.

name: Eridanus
myth: The River, A great celestial river, the 6th largest constellation. Winds from Orion's foot to deep south.
keyStars: Achernar (flattest known star, spins so fast it bulges)

name: Fornax
myth: The Furnace, A chemical furnace. Faint but contains the Fornax galaxy cluster.

name: Sculptor
myth: The Sculptor, A sculptor's studio. Contains the south galactic pole.

name: Cetus
myth: The Sea Monster, Sent to devour Andromeda. Contains Mira, the prototype long-period variable star.

name: Triangulum
myth: The Triangle, One of the smallest constellations. Contains M33, the Triangulum Galaxy.
objects: Triangulum Galaxy (M33, 3rd largest in Local Group)

name: Piscis Austrinus
myth: The Southern Fish, Drinks water poured by Aquarius. Fomalhaut is one of the Royal Stars of Persia.

name: Ophiuchus
myth: The Serpent Bearer, Asclepius, the healer. The Sun actually passes through this constellation, though it's not a zodiac sign.
funFact: Barnard's Star, the fastest-moving star in the sky, is in Ophiuchus (6 ly away).

name: Serpens
myth: The Serpent, The only constellation split in two (Caput and Cauda), held by Ophiuchus.

name: Auriga
myth: The Charioteer, A chariot driver holding a goat (Capella) and kids.
keyStars: Capella (6th brightest, a quadruple star system)

name: Canes Venatici
myth: The Hunting Dogs, Two dogs held by Boötes.

name: Coma Berenices
myth: Berenice's Hair, Queen Berenice's locks, cut as an offering. Contains the north galactic pole.

name: Scutum
myth: The Shield, Sobieski's Shield. Small but rich in Milky Way star clouds.

name: Sagitta
myth: The Arrow, The 3rd smallest constellation, an arrow shot by Hercules.

name: Vulpecula
myth: The Fox, A small faint constellation in the Milky Way.

name: Delphinus
myth: The Dolphin, A dolphin that helped Poseidon find Amphitrite. Star names are "Nicolaus" and "Venator" reversed.

name: Equuleus
myth: The Little Horse, 2nd smallest constellation, a foal.

name: Lacerta
myth: The Lizard, A zigzag of faint stars between Cygnus and Andromeda.

name: Monoceros
myth: The Unicorn, A faint constellation in the Milky Way between Orion's dogs.

name: Sextans
myth: The Sextant, An astronomical instrument, very faint.

name: Leo Minor
myth: The Little Lion, A small faint lion between Leo and Ursa Major.

name: Lynx
myth: The Lynx, Named because you need lynx-like eyesight to see it.

name: Camelopardalis
myth: The Giraffe, A large but very faint constellation near the north pole.

name: Triangulum Australe
myth: The Southern Triangle, A bright, compact southern constellation.

name: Norma
myth: The Square, A carpenter's level. Lies in the Milky Way.

name: Corona Australis
myth: The Southern Crown, A small arc of stars below Sagittarius.

name: Circinus
myth: The Compass, A draughtsman's compasses, near Alpha Centauri.

name: Musca
myth: The Fly, A small constellation just south of Crux.

name: Chamaeleon
myth: The Chameleon, A faint southern constellation near the celestial pole.

name: Volans
myth: The Flying Fish, A flying fish of the southern sky.

name: Pictor
myth: The Painter's Easel, Contains Beta Pictoris, famous for its protoplanetary disk.

name: Dorado
myth: The Swordfish, Contains most of the Large Magellanic Cloud.
objects: Tarantula Nebula (30 Doradus, most active star-forming region known)

name: Reticulum
myth: The Reticle, A crosshair in a telescope eyepiece.

name: Horologium
myth: The Clock, A pendulum clock, very faint.

name: Caelum
myth: The Chisel, An engraving tool, one of the faintest constellations.

name: Mensa
myth: The Table Mountain, Named for Table Mountain in Cape Town. Contains part of the LMC. The only constellation named after a real place.

name: Octans
myth: The Octant, Contains the south celestial pole (no bright pole star).

name: Apus
myth: The Bird of Paradise, A colorful tropical bird.

name: Telescopium
myth: The Telescope, A small faint constellation south of Corona Australis.

name: Microscopium
myth: The Microscope, A small scientific instrument constellation.

name: Indus
myth: The Indian, Represents a native figure, near the south celestial pole.

name: Antlia
myth: The Air Pump, A pneumatic pump. Very faint.

name: Pyxis
myth: The Compass, A mariner's compass from the ship Argo.
